topics.title: Linked List
topics.story: You're at an airport following a treasure hunt. Each clue doesn't give you the treasure — it tells you where the next clue is. That's a linked list: each node holds data and a pointer to the next node. No random access. No jumping to clue #5 without following 1→2→3→4→5.
topics.analogy: A treasure hunt where each clue leads to the next — no map, just one clue at a time. Adding a new clue is easy (slip it anywhere), but finding clue #50 means walking the whole chain.
topics.memoryAnchor: LINKED = each node is LINKED to the next. The last node points to null — the treasure chest.
topics.firstPrinciples: A linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node contains data and a reference to the next node. Time: O(1) insert at known position, O(n) search. Space: O(n). Unlike arrays, nodes are scattered in memory — no indexing, but no shifting on insert/delete.
topics.bruteForce: For most operations (find, delete by value): scan every node sequentially — O(n). For reverse: collect all values, recreate list. Always valid but never fast.
topics.optimizedApproach: Two-pointer (runner) technique for cycle detection, middle finding, kth-from-end. Sentinel/dummy head node to simplify insert/delete at the start. Reverse in-place with three pointers (prev, curr, next).
topics.whenToUse: When you need O(1) insert/delete at the front or at a known position, and sequential access is acceptable. Used in LRU Cache, adjacency lists, undo history.

examples.title: Reverse a Linked List
examples.explanation: Three pointers: prev (reversed portion), curr (current node), next (save before breaking link). Iterate until curr is null.

problems.title: Reverse a Linked List
problems.description:
Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the reversed list.

**Example:**
Input: 1→2→3→4→5
Output: 5→4→3→2→1

**Example 2:**
Input: 1→2
Output: 2→1
problems.hint1: Use three pointers: prev, curr, and next. What does 'next' need to save before you break curr.next?
problems.hint2: In each step: (1) save curr.next in a temp variable, (2) point curr.next to prev, (3) advance prev to curr, (4) advance curr to saved next.
problems.hint3: Initialize prev = null, curr = head. Loop while curr != null. Return prev at the end — it's the new head.
problems.editorial: Iterative: maintain prev=null, curr=head. In each step save curr.next, flip curr.next=prev, advance both. O(n) time, O(1) space. Recursive: reverse(head.next), then head.next.next=head, head.next=null. O(n) space from call stack.

problems.title: Detect Cycle in Linked List
problems.description:
Given the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle. A cycle exists if there is some node in the list that can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer.

**Example:**
Input: head = [3,2,0,-4], pos = 1 (tail connects to node at index 1)
Output: true

**Example 2:**
Input: head = [1,2], pos = -1 (no cycle)
Output: false
problems.hint1: Can you use two pointers moving at different speeds? What happens if one moves twice as fast?
problems.hint2: Floyd's Cycle Detection: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. If there's a cycle, fast will 'lap' slow and they'll meet.
problems.hint3: If fast == null or fast.next == null, there's no cycle (fast reached the end). If slow == fast, a cycle exists.
problems.editorial: Floyd's Cycle Detection: O(n) time, O(1) space. slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If cycle exists, fast laps slow and they meet inside the cycle. Alternative: HashSet of visited nodes — O(n) space.

problems.title: Find Middle of Linked List
problems.description:
Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node. If there are two middle nodes, return the second middle node.

**Example:**
Input: [1,2,3,4,5] → Output: node 3
Input: [1,2,3,4,5,6] → Output: node 4 (second middle)
problems.hint1: Can you reach the middle in one pass without knowing the length?
problems.hint2: Use two pointers. When the fast pointer reaches the end, where is the slow pointer?
problems.hint3: slow=head, fast=head. Move slow 1 step, fast 2 steps. When fast==null or fast.next==null, slow is at the middle.
problems.editorial: Runner technique: fast at 2x speed. When fast hits the end, slow is at floor(n/2)+1 — which is exactly the second middle for even-length lists. O(n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problems.description:
Merge two sorted linked lists and return it as a sorted list.

**Example:**
Input: l1 = [1,2,4], l2 = [1,3,4]
Output: [1,1,2,3,4,4]
problems.hint1: Use a dummy head node to avoid special-casing the head of the merged list.
problems.hint2: Compare l1.val and l2.val. Attach the smaller one to your result, and advance that pointer.
problems.hint3: After the loop (one list exhausted), attach the remaining nodes of the other list — no need to iterate, just point tail.next = remaining.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ length of each list ≤ 50 | -100 ≤ Node.val ≤ 100 | Both lists are sorted in non-decreasing order
problems.editorial: Dummy head simplifies code — no special case for empty result. Iteratively pick smaller head. After loop, at most one list remains — attach entire tail. O(m+n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End
problems.description:
Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.

**Example:**
Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5], n = 2
Output: [1,2,3,5]  (removed 4, which is 2nd from end)
problems.hint1: Can you find the nth from end in one pass? How would two pointers with a gap of n help?
problems.hint2: Advance the fast pointer n+1 steps first (using a dummy head). Then move both slow and fast together until fast is null. Now slow.next is the node to remove.
problems.hint3: Use a dummy node before head so you can delete the head node without special-casing. slow starts at dummy, fast starts at dummy. Advance fast n+1 steps, then move both until fast==null.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 30 | 0 ≤ Node.val ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ n ≤ number of nodes
problems.editorial: Two-pointer with n+1 gap (advance fast n+1 steps, not n, because we need slow to stop at the node BEFORE the one to delete). Dummy node handles head deletion cleanly. O(n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: Check if Linked List is Palindrome
problems.description:
Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome.

**Example:**
Input: [1,2,2,1] → Output: true
Input: [1,2] → Output: false
problems.hint1: How can you compare the first half with the second half without using extra space?
problems.hint2: Find the middle, reverse the second half in-place, then compare both halves node by node.
problems.hint3: Steps: (1) find middle using fast/slow, (2) reverse from mid to end, (3) compare head and reversed-half simultaneously, (4) optionally restore the list.
problems.editorial: Three phases: find middle O(n), reverse second half O(n), compare O(n). Total O(n) time, O(1) space. The in-place reversal is the key — avoid the O(n) array approach.

problems.title: Add Two Numbers as Linked Lists
problems.description:
Two non-empty linked lists represent two non-negative integers in reverse order (ones digit first). Add the numbers and return the sum as a linked list.

**Example:**
Input: l1 = [2,4,3], l2 = [5,6,4]
Output: [7,0,8]  (342 + 465 = 807)
problems.hint1: Simulate grade-school addition: add corresponding digits plus carry. What do you output if both lists are exhausted but carry is 1?
problems.hint2: Use a dummy head. Loop while l1 != null OR l2 != null OR carry != 0. Take l1.val (0 if null), l2.val (0 if null), add them + carry.
problems.hint3: sum = (l1?.val ?? 0) + (l2?.val ?? 0) + carry. New node value = sum % 10. carry = sum / 10. Advance l1 and l2 if not null.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 100 | 0 ≤ Node.val ≤ 9 | No leading zeros except the number 0 itself
problems.editorial: Simulate addition column by column. The `|| carry != 0` in the loop condition handles the final carry digit (e.g., 999 + 1 = 1000). O(max(m,n)) time and space.

problems.title: LRU Cache
problems.description:
Design a data structure that follows the LRU (Least Recently Used) cache eviction policy.

Implement LRUCache(int capacity), int get(int key), void put(int key, int value).
- get: Return value if key exists, else -1. Mark as recently used.
- put: Insert or update. If capacity exceeded, evict the LRU item.

Both operations must be O(1).
problems.hint1: You need O(1) lookup AND O(1) delete-from-any-position AND O(1) insert-at-front. Which data structures give you each?
problems.hint2: HashMap gives O(1) lookup by key. Doubly linked list gives O(1) insert/delete at any position (if you have the node reference). Combine them.
problems.hint3: HashMap maps key → DLL node. The DLL is ordered by recency (head=most recent, tail=LRU). On get/put: move node to head. On eviction: remove tail. Use dummy head and tail sentinels.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ capacity ≤ 3000 | 0 ≤ key ≤ 10^4 | 0 ≤ value ≤ 10^5 | At most 2*10^5 calls to get and put
problems.editorial: HashMap + Doubly Linked List. HashMap gives O(1) key lookup. DLL gives O(1) move-to-front and O(1) remove-from-tail. Dummy sentinel head/tail nodes eliminate null checks. Classic system design + DSA hybrid problem.

problems.title: Copy List with Random Pointer
problems.description:
A linked list is given where each node has a `next` pointer and a `random` pointer (can point to any node or null). Return a deep copy of the list.

**Example:**
Input: [[7,null],[13,0],[11,4],[10,2],[1,0]]
(each pair is [val, random_index])
Output: Deep copy with same structure
problems.hint1: The challenge is random pointers — you can't set them until all nodes are created. How do you map old nodes to new nodes?
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap: old_node → new_node. First pass: create all new nodes and populate the map. Second pass: set next and random pointers using the map.
problems.hint3: For each old node: newNode.next = map.get(old.next), newNode.random = map.get(old.random). Both can be null — map.get(null) returns null safely.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ n ≤ 1000 | -10^4 ≤ Node.val ≤ 10^4 | Node.random is null or points to some node in the list
problems.editorial: Two-pass HashMap: O(n) time, O(n) space. Pass 1 creates all clone nodes. Pass 2 wires next and random using the map. O(1) space alternative: interleave clones between original nodes (weave technique), set randoms, then unweave.

problems.title: Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List
problems.description:
A doubly linked list may have nodes with a `child` pointer to another doubly linked list. Flatten the list so all nodes appear in a single-level list. The child list should be inserted between the node and its next.

**Example:**
1 - 2 - 3 - 4 - 5
        |child
        7 - 8 - 9
Output: 1 - 2 - 3 - 7 - 8 - 9 - 4 - 5
problems.hint1: When you find a node with a child, you need to splice the child list between the current node and its next. What information do you need to save before unlinking?
problems.hint2: Save curr.next (the node after curr). Connect curr → child. Connect end of child list → saved next. Clear curr.child.
problems.hint3: Use a stack or recursion: when you encounter a child, push next to a stack and follow the child. When you hit null, pop from the stack to continue.
problems.editorial: Iterative in-place: when child found, find child's tail O(child length), splice. Total O(n) since each node visited at most twice. Stack-based approach: O(n) time, O(depth) space — push next before following child.
